title: Interview Templates
description: Drill the most common algorithmic templates in Go. Each pattern provides a reusable skeleton you can adapt to dozens of problems — recognizing and applying them quickly becomes second nature.

sections.title: Sliding Window
sections.explanation: The sliding window technique maintains a fixed- or variable-size window over a slice, sliding it forward one element at a time. Instead of recalculating from scratch, you update by subtracting the leaving element and adding the entering one. This reduces O(n*k) brute-force to O(n). Interviewers expect you to recognize this pattern immediately for subarray/substring problems.

exercise.prompt: Complete the sliding window function to find the maximum sum of any contiguous subarray of size k. Print the result for nums = [2, 3, 1, 5, 4, 2] with k = 3.

validation.message: Max sum subarray of size 3 is [3,1,5]+[1,5,4]? Actually [1,5,4]=10, [5,4,2]=11
Subtract nums[i-k] and update best when window exceeds it

exercise.hint: Subtract the element leaving the window with nums[i-k]. Update best when window > best.

sections.title: Binary Search
sections.explanation: Binary search halves the search space each iteration using two pointers, left and right. The template uses **left <= right** as the loop condition and calculates mid as **(left + right) / 2**. Move left to mid+1 or right to mid-1 based on comparison. Interviewers test edge cases and off-by-one errors, so drill this until automatic.

exercise.prompt: Complete the binary search function. Print the result for nums = [2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 12] and target = 10.

validation.message: Use left <= right and move by mid +/- 1

exercise.hint: Loop while left <= right. Move left to mid + 1 when too small, right to mid - 1 when too large.

sections.title: Two Pointers
sections.explanation: The two-pointer technique uses one pointer at the start and one at the end of a sorted array, moving inward based on a condition. For pair-sum: if sum is too small, advance left; if too large, retreat right. This is O(n) time, O(1) space — a tradeoff interviewers love.

exercise.prompt: Complete the two-pointer function to find indices whose values sum to target in a sorted array. Print the result for nums = [1, 2, 4, 6, 8, 11] and target = 10.

validation.message: Increment left when sum too small, decrement right when too large

exercise.hint: When s < target, left++ to increase the sum. When s > target, right-- to decrease it.

sections.title: Prefix Sum
sections.explanation: A prefix sum array stores cumulative sums: **prefix[i+1] = prefix[i] + nums[i]**. Any range sum `nums[l..r]` becomes **prefix[r+1] - prefix[l]** in O(1). Building the prefix is O(n). This pattern is a building block for subarray-sum, running-average, and 2D-matrix problems.

exercise.prompt: Build a prefix sum array for [2, 5, 1, 7, 3]. Print the sum of elements from index 1 to 3 (inclusive), which should be 5+1+7 = 13.

validation.message: Use prefix[i] to build and prefix[r+1] - prefix[l] for range query

exercise.hint: prefix[i+1] = prefix[i] + nums[i] builds the array. Range sum = prefix[r+1] - prefix[l].

sections.title: Backtracking
sections.explanation: Backtracking explores every candidate solution: choose an option, recurse deeper, then undo the choice. In Go, use a closure with a shared path slice. **Critical**: copy the path before appending to results since slices share memory. Prune early for efficiency. This pattern solves permutations, combinations, and constraint problems.

exercise.prompt: Generate all combinations of size 2 from [1, 2, 3, 4] using backtracking. Only add to result when path length equals 2. Print the result.

validation.message: Check path length, copy before storing, recurse with i+1

exercise.hint: Base case: len(path) == 2. Copy path before appending to result. Recurse with backtrack(i+1, path).
